1: im-tashuv Yisrael ... im tashuv elai (ʾim-tāšûb yiśrāʾēl ... ʾim tāšûb ʾēlay): 'If you return, O Israel ... if you return to me.' Shuv (return/repent) is the single most important word in Jeremiah's vocabulary, occurring over 100 times in the book. The double conditional here — return generally, and then specifically 'to me' — captures Jeremiah's insistence that repentance is not ritual or geographic but relational and directional: it is a turning toward YHWH himself. The negative formulation 'you will not be driven away' (lo-tanud) makes exile the default outcome if the return does not happen.
2: nishbata chai YHWH be-emet be-mishpat uve-tzedaqah (nišbāʿtā ḥay Yhwh beʾĕmet bĕmišpāṭ ûbĕṣĕdāqāh): 'You shall swear, 'As the LORD lives,' in truth, in justice, and in righteousness.' The three attributes — emet (truth/faithfulness), mishpat (justice), tzedaqah (righteousness) — are the three pillars of covenant loyalty and the same triad Hosea uses for the renewed covenant in Hos 2:19-20. An oath 'as the LORD lives' is the highest covenant speech act; for it to be valid, the swearer must embody the same qualities that characterize YHWH himself. The nations blessing themselves by YHWH (v. 2b) anticipates the Abrahamic blessing (Gen 12:3), available only when Israel lives as a covenant people.
3: niru lachem nir veal-tizru el-qotzim (nîrû lākem nîr wĕʾal-tizrĕʿû ʾel-qôṣîm): 'Break up your fallow ground and do not sow among thorns.' The agricultural metaphor for spiritual preparation: niru (break up / plow fallow ground) uses the same root as nir (untilled ground). Sowing seed without preparing the soil — specifically without removing thorns — wastes the seed and guarantees a choked harvest. Jesus's parable of the sower (Mark 4:7, 18-19) applies this same image: seed sown among thorns is choked by cares and riches. Jeremiah's agricultural metaphor anticipates the parable's diagnostic of unrepared hearts.
4: himolu l-YHWH vasiru arlos levavchem (himmōlû lYhwh wĕsîrû ʿorlôt lĕbabkem): 'Circumcise yourselves to the LORD and remove the foreskins of your hearts.' Orlah (foreskin/uncircumcision) applied to the heart appears also in Lev 26:41 and Deut 10:16 and 30:6. The physical sign of the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17) becomes the demand for an inner transformation: the outer cut must correspond to an inner removal of the obstruction to covenant faithfulness. Paul's argument in Rom 2:28-29 — 'true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit, not the letter' — is the NT formulation of exactly what Jeremiah here demands; Paul is not inventing a new concept but drawing out the Jeremianic logic that inner circumcision is what the outer sign was always pointing toward.
5: tiq'u shofar ba-aretz (tiqʿû šôpār bāʾāreṣ): 'Sound the trumpet throughout the land.' The shofar (ram's horn trumpet) served as both a military alarm and a covenant-assembly signal (cf. Num 10). Its sounding inaugurated fasts, new moons, and the Day of Atonement (Lev 25:9). Here its covenant-assembly function is perverted into a war-alarm: the sacred instrument that called Israel to worship now calls them to flee. Gathering ('assemble together') is the gathering not for worship but for survival.
6: sera' nesi el-tzion (śĕʾû-nes ʾel-ṣiyyôn): 'Raise the signal flag toward Zion.' The signal (nes) or standard was a rallying point visible from a distance — associated with Isaiah's eschatological visions of nations streaming to Zion (Isa 11:12; 49:22). Here the same signal is used in reverse: not a beacon drawing the nations to peace but a marker pointing to the city under siege. The disaster from the north (tsafon) is Jeremiah's standard designation for Babylon, though at the time of composition (ca. 627-605 BCE) the identity of the northern power was not yet fully determined.
7: alah aryeh misuvko (ʿālāh ʾaryēh miśśubkô): 'The lion has come out of his thicket.' The lion is one of Jeremiah's most repeated images for Babylon (4:7; 5:6; 25:38; 49:19; 50:17, 44). Mashchit goyim (destroyer of nations): a title that appears again at 6:26 and 51:25, where YHWH himself uses it of Babylon. The theological move is significant: Babylon, the destroyer of nations, is YHWH's instrument — a truth that makes Jeremiah deeply unpopular (cf. 27:6: 'I have given all these lands to Nebuchadnezzar my servant').
8: chagru sakim sepedu u-veilu (ḥigĕrû śaqqîm sipĕdû ûbĕkû): 'Put on sackcloth, wail and howl.' The lamentation ritual vocabulary: saq (sackcloth, coarse goat-hair fabric worn as a sign of mourning or penitence), saphad (wail/lament in a formal mourning sense), yalal (howl, the animal-like cry of extreme grief). The three verbs together evoke the communal mourning rite, but Jeremiah uses them proleptically — before the disaster, not after — demanding mourning as appropriate response to the already-determined judgment.
9: lev-hamelech yoved vylev hasarim (lēb-hammelek yōbēd wĕlēb haśśārîm): 'The king's heart will fail and the courage of the officials.' Lev (heart) in Hebrew is the center of will, courage, and decision-making — not primarily emotion as in English. The 'heart failing' idiom means loss of will and determination (cf. Deut 20:8; Josh 2:11: 'our hearts melted'). The priests are 'horrified' (shamem, the same root as the 'abomination of desolation') and the prophets 'stunned' (tamah). The three leadership groups — king, priests, prophets — all fail simultaneously.
10: hissata et-ha'am hazeh veeth Yerushalaim lemor shalom yihyeh lachem (hiššêtā ʾet-hāʿam hazzeh wĕʾet-yĕrûšālaim lēmōr šālôm yihyeh lākem): 'You have truly deceived this people and Jerusalem, saying, 'You shall have peace.'' This is one of the most theologically shocking verses in the prophets: Jeremiah appears to accuse YHWH himself of deception. The verb nasha (deceive/beguile) is used of Eve's deception in Gen 3:13. Various interpretations: (1) Jeremiah is quoting what false prophets have claimed YHWH said; (2) Jeremiah is expressing his own anguished confusion (cf. 20:7: 'O LORD, you deceived me'); (3) Jeremiah reflects the genuine ambiguity of how to read YHWH's past covenant promises of peace when disaster comes. In context, the verse more likely continues the lament: YHWH allowed false prophets to give false assurances.
11: ruach tsah shefahim bamidbar (rûaḥ ṣāḥ šĕpāʿîm bammidbar): 'A scorching wind from the bare heights of the wilderness.' The shefahim (bare heights/desolate heights) are the exposed hilltops of the Judean desert — uninhabited and wind-swept. The east wind (qadim, from 4:11-12's implied direction) is the fierce desert wind (sirocco) that withers vegetation and brings oppressive heat. The key distinction: this is 'not to winnow grain or to cleanse' — ordinary agricultural winds serve those purposes, but this wind is for judgment only, overwhelming and purposeless from an economic standpoint.
12: atah gam ani adaber mishpatim otam (ʾattāh gam-ʾănî ʾădabbēr mišpāṭîm ʾôtām): 'Now I will pronounce judgment against them.' The switch to first-person divine speech mid-oracle. Mishpatim (judgments/legal decisions) carries the force of a court verdict: YHWH is both the aggrieved party and the judge, delivering a forensic sentence. The phrase 'at my command' (mimeni, 'from me') establishes that even the devastating wind of v. 11 is under divine authority.
13: hinei kashananiyim ya'aleh (hinnēh kaʿănānîm yaʿăleh): 'Look — he advances like storm clouds.' The comparison to storm clouds, chariots like whirlwinds (sûphah), and horses swifter than eagles (nesharim) builds an overwhelming image of unstoppable speed. The eagle (nesher) was the fastest creature in ancient Near Eastern imagination (cf. Deut 28:49; Hab 1:8). The exclamation 'we are doomed' (oy lanu, 'woe to us') is in the mouth of the people who see the invasion coming and can do nothing.
14: kabsi mirah libech Yerushalaim l'ma'an tivashah'i (kabbĕsî mērāʿāh libbēk yĕrûšālaim lĕmaʿan tiwwāšĕʿî): 'Wash the evil from your heart, O Jerusalem, so that you may be saved.' The verb kabas (wash/launder) is used for ritual cleansing of clothes and of persons (Lev 13-14; Ps 51:2: 'wash me thoroughly from my iniquity'). The linkage of 'evil from your heart' with 'so that you may be saved' makes salvation conditional on inner moral washing — not on external ritual compliance. The machshevot aven (wicked schemes, literally 'plans of wickedness') that need to be expelled are the deliberate strategies of unfaithfulness, not accidental lapses.
15: qol magid midan uma'avim mashmi'a aven (qôl maggîd middān ûmaśmîaʿ ʾāwen mehar ʾeprayim): 'A voice proclaims from Dan and announces disaster from Mount Ephraim.' Dan, the northernmost city of Israel, and Mount Ephraim (the central highlands) mark the geographic progression of bad news traveling south toward Jerusalem. The voice announcing disaster moves through the tribal territory like a wave — each reporting post relays the news of the incoming invasion. This geographical sequence would be familiar to Judean hearers who knew the traditional tribal territory names.
16: hazkiru lagoyim hinei hashmi'u al-Yerushalaim (hazzekîrû laggôyim hinnēh haššmîʿû ʿal-yĕrûšālaim): 'Tell the nations; announce concerning Jerusalem: Besiegers are coming from a distant land.' The command to 'tell the nations' inverts the expected missionary direction: instead of the nations learning of YHWH's greatness from Israel, the nations are called as witnesses to Israel's humiliation. The notzrim (besiegers/watchers) coming from a distant land echoes Deut 28:49-50's covenant curse: 'the LORD will bring against you a nation from far away, from the end of the earth' — Jeremiah is announcing the arrival of exactly what Moses warned would happen if the covenant were broken.
17: ki-marat bi neum YHWH (kî mārāt bî nĕʾum Yhwh): 'Because she has rebelled against me, declares the LORD.' The single word marah (rebel/be bitter/be obstinate) explains the entire invasion. The root conveys active, willful defiance — not mere failure but deliberate turning against. The same word appears in the wilderness narratives for Israel's rebellion at Meribah (Num 20:10, 24). Jeremiah consistently returns to the covenantal diagnosis: the catastrophe is not fate or Babylonian power but the logical consequence of Israel's chosen rebellion.
18: darkech uma'alalayich asu elleh lach (darkêk ûmaʿălālayik ʿāśû ʾēlleh lāk): 'Your conduct and your actions have brought this on you.' The two-word pair derekh (way/conduct, habitual path of life) and ma'alalim (acts/deeds, individual actions) covers both character and behavior. This is the prophetic announcements of 'consequential connection': the judgment is not arbitrary but organically connected to the sin. Ki mar ki naga ad-libbech (how bitter it has pierced to your very heart) — the same word mar (bitter) used of Miriam (Exod 15:23) and Ruth (Ruth 1:20). The bitterness of the punishment mirrors the bitterness of the rebellion.
19: meai meai aholelah qirot libbi (mēʿay mēʿay ʾôḥîlāh qîrôt libbî): 'My anguish, my anguish! I writhe in pain! The chambers of my heart are throbbing.' This is one of the most raw expressions of the prophetic pathos in all of Scripture. Meai (intestines/bowels/anguish) is the visceral center of emotion in Hebrew anthropology — the seat of deep feeling (cf. Phil 1:8, 'the affections/bowels of Christ Jesus' in the KJV). The doubling (meai meai) is the Hebrew intensifier expressing overwhelmed distress. Whether the speaker is Jeremiah or YHWH through Jeremiah is deliberately ambiguous — the prophet and the divine pathos merge. Hemeh hamah (throbbing/churning) of the heart's chambers gives a physical image of cardiac palpitation.
20: shever al-shever niqra ki shuddedah kol-ha'aretz (šeber ʿal-šeber niqrāʾ kî šuddĕdāh kol-hāʾāreṣ): 'Crash after crash — the whole land is laid waste.' The rhetorical device of repetition (sheber al-shever) captures the cascading nature of the disaster: each wave of destruction brings a new one. Sheber (breaking/destruction) is the covenant curse word for catastrophic defeat (Amos 6:6: 'the ruin of Joseph'; Isa 30:26: the healing of 'the breaking'). The word also appears in the lament context as the 'wound' of the people (8:21; 14:17). The sudden destruction of the tent (ohel) and curtains (yeri'ot) in an instant echoes the nomadic vulnerability of life without divine protection.
21: ad-matai er'eh-nes eshma qol shofar (ʿad-mātay ʾerʾeh-nes ʾešmaʿ qôl šôpār): 'How long must I see the battle standard and hear the blare of the trumpet?' The ad-matai (how long?) is the classic lament formula of the Psalms (Ps 6:3; 13:1-2; 79:5; 80:4). Jeremiah adopts the Psalmic complaint language to express the exhaustion of sustained prophetic intercession: he has been seeing the vision of disaster and proclaiming it, and still the people do not return. The prophet's endurance is itself becoming a theme of the book (cf. the 'Confessions,' chs. 11-20).
22: ki evili ami oti lo yad'u banim sechel hem velo nevonim hem (kî ʾĕwîl ʿammî ʾôtî lōʾ yādāʿû bānîm sĕkālîm hēm wĕlōʾ nĕbōnîm hēm): 'For my people are foolish — they do not know me; they are stupid children; they have no understanding.' Three synonymous indictments: evil (foolish, morally senseless — the one who 'says in his heart there is no God' in Ps 14:1 is a naval, a related word); lo yad'u (they do not know me — covenant knowledge of YHWH is the core deficit); sekalim (stupid, those who act without wisdom). The irony of v. 22b: 'they are clever at doing evil, but they do not know how to do good.' The same cognitive capacity that should be used for discerning the good is instead refined for evil.
23: ra'iti et-ha'aretz vehinei tohu va-vohu (rāʾîtî ʾet-hāʾāreṣ wĕhinnēh tōhû wābōhû): 'I looked at the earth and it was formless and void.' The phrase tohu va-vohu appears only twice in the OT: here and in Gen 1:2, where it describes the uncreated earth before the Spirit moved over it. Jeremiah's vision is a deliberate reversal of the creation narrative: vv. 23-26 systematically undo the first six days of creation. Light is removed (v. 23 / Day 1), the mountains shake (v. 24 / Day 3), birds flee (v. 25 / Day 5), humans disappear (v. 25 / Day 6), and fertile land returns to desert (v. 26 / Day 3). Covenant judgment is cosmological un-creation: without YHWH's presence and word, creation reverts to pre-creation chaos. This is one of the most theologically profound images in the prophets.
24: ra'iti vehinei heharim ra'ashu vegavot hitqosheshu (rāʾîtî wĕhinnēh hehārîm rāʿāšû wĕkol-haggĕbāʿôt hitqôšāšû): 'I looked at the mountains and they were quaking; all the hills were swaying.' The quaking mountains (ra'ash) are a standard theophanic marker — YHWH's approach causes mountains to tremble (Ps 18:7; Isa 5:25; Nah 1:5). Here the quaking signals not a saving theophany (as at Sinai) but a judgment theophany. The four-fold 'I looked and behold' structure (vv. 23-26) mimics the creation narrative's 'and God saw' — but what is seen is dis-creation.
25: ra'iti vehinei eyn ha'adam (rāʾîtî wĕhinnēh ʾên hāʾādām): 'I looked — there was no human being, and all the birds of the sky had flown away.' Ha'adam (the human / humankind) echoes Gen 1:26-27 where God creates ha'adam in his image on the sixth day. The absence of humans and birds reverses Days 5 and 6 of creation (birds on Day 5, animals and humans on Day 6). The emptied earth — once teeming with life — has returned to the formlessness of Gen 1:2. The birds' departure signals that the ecosystem has collapsed: animals flee a land where YHWH's presence has withdrawn.
26: ra'iti vehinei hakarmel hamidbar (rāʾîtî wĕhinnēh hakkarmel hammidbar): 'I looked — the fertile land was a desert; all its cities lay in ruins before the LORD.' Karmel (fertile land, literally 'the garden land' or orchard — also the famous mountain range) in contrast with midbar (wilderness/desert) reverses the land's transformation under covenant blessing. The phrase 'before the LORD, because of his fierce anger' (mipnei YHWH mipnei charon appo) names the divine source: the de-creation is not Babylon's achievement but YHWH's judgment enacted through Babylon.
27: kalah lo eseh (kālāh lōʾ ʾeśeh): 'I will not make a complete end.' This brief clause — 'a complete end I will not make' — is Jeremiah's key qualifying restraint on the vision of total destruction (repeated at 5:10, 18; 30:11; 46:28). Kalah (complete end/annihilation) is the strongest term for total destruction. The qualification does not soften the judgment but establishes the covenant basis: Israel is not destroyed like Sodom (though the comparison is implicit) because the Abrahamic promises remain in force. This is the theological nerve of Jeremiah's entire ministry — YHWH's judgment is severe but bounded by covenant faithfulness.
28: dibarti zamoti velo-nichamti velo-ashuv mimmenu (dibbartî zammōtî wĕlōʾ-niḥamtî wĕlōʾ-ʾāšûb mimmennû): 'For I have spoken, I have decided, and I will not relent or turn back from it.' The verb nacham (relent/repent/be comforted) appears in the famous 'divine relenting' passages (Exod 32:14; Jonah 3:10; Joel 2:13). Here YHWH declares he will not relent — the judgment is determined. The earth's mourning (avlah) and the darkening sky anticipate apocalyptic and eschatological imagery; the earth becomes a mourner at YHWH's decree.
29: kol-ha'ir nos'ah miqol happarash veroeh qeshet (kol-hāʿîr nōśāʾāh miqqôl happārāš wĕrōkēb qāšet): 'Every city is abandoned; they flee from the noise of horsemen and archers.' The universal abandonment (kol-ha'ir, every city) captures the scale of the flight. The thickets (avim) and rocks (sela'im) where people hide echo Isa 2:10-19's Day of YHWH scene: 'enter the rocks and hide in the ground from before the terror of the LORD.' Hiding in nature is futile refuge — the same theme.
30: veatt shad'udah ma-ta'asi (wĕʾatt šādûdāh mah-taʿăśî): 'And you, O desolate one — what will you do?' The 'you' is personified Jerusalem, addressed as a woman abandoned by her lovers. The three futile actions — dressing in crimson, adorning with gold, painting eyes with kohl — are the preparations of a woman attempting to attract allies (lovers/nations). Puch (kohl, the black eye-paint made from antimony) was common cosmetic preparation. The lovers' rejection ('they want your life') unmasks the transactional nature of the political alliances Jerusalem was pursuing instead of returning to YHWH.
31: qol bat-tzion titayapeyach tifros kapeiha (qôl bat-ṣiyyôn tityappēaḥ tipĕrôś kappêhā): 'The cry of Daughter Zion, gasping and reaching out her hands.' The chapter closes with the image of personified Jerusalem as a woman in labor — the same 'birth pangs' imagery used in eschatological contexts throughout Scripture (Isa 13:8; 26:17; Mic 4:9-10; John 16:21; 1 Thess 5:3). Bat Tzion (Daughter Zion) is the feminine personification of the city as both a collective and a person in covenant relationship with YHWH. The 'reaching out her hands' (paras kapeiha) is the gesture of desperate supplication — the same posture as prayer (Exod 9:29; 1 Kings 8:22; Isa 1:15).
